annotations.label: Cauchy sequence (informal)

anchor.snippet: a Cauchy sequence is a sequence whose elements become arbitrarily close to each other as the sequence progresses

annotations.note: `CauchySeq u := Cauchy (atTop.map u)` is Mathlib's general uniform-space formalization of the Cauchy property.

annotations.label: Square roots not Cauchy

anchor.snippet: It is not sufficient for each term to become arbitrarily close to the preceding term.

annotations.note: This is an informal cautionary remark with no specific formal statement in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: It is not sufficient for each term to become arbitrarily close to the preceding term.

annotations.label: Cauchy sequence of real numbers

anchor.snippet: of real numbers is called a Cauchy sequence if for every positive real number

annotations.note: `IsCauSeq abv f := ∀ ε>0, ∃i, ∀j≥i, abv (f j - f i) < ε` gives the real (absolute-value) ε–N Cauchy definition as a special case.

anchors.snippet: of real numbers is called a Cauchy sequence if for every positive real number

annotations.label: Truncated decimal expansions

anchor.snippet: the sequence of truncated decimal expansions of r forms a Cauchy sequence

annotations.note: No named Mathlib result asserts that the truncated-decimal sequence of a real is Cauchy.

annotations.label: Modulus of Cauchy convergence

anchor.snippet: then a modulus of Cauchy convergence for the sequence is a function

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated `modulus of Cauchy convergence` definition packaging the function N(ε).

annotations.label: Modulus implies Cauchy

anchor.snippet: Any sequence with a modulus of Cauchy convergence is a Cauchy sequence.

annotations.note: Since the modulus notion is absent, this implication is not stated as such in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regular Cauchy sequence

anchor.snippet: Regular Cauchy sequences are sequences with a given modulus of Cauchy convergence

annotations.note: No `regular Cauchy sequence` (fixed modulus 1/n) definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equivalent to regular Cauchy sequence

anchor.snippet: Any Cauchy sequence with a modulus of Cauchy convergence is equivalent to a regular Cauchy sequence

annotations.note: This depends on the unformalized regular/modulus notions, so it has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Cauchy sequence in a metric space

anchor.snippet: is Cauchy, if for every positive real number

annotations.note: `Metric.cauchySeq_iff` characterizes `CauchySeq u` by `∀ ε>0, ∃N, ∀ m,n≥N, dist (u m) (u n) < ε` in any (pseudo)metric space.

anchors.snippet: is Cauchy, if for every positive real number

annotations.label: Complete metric space

anchor.snippet: in which every Cauchy sequence converges to an element of X is called complete

annotations.note: `CompleteSpace` (every Cauchy filter converges) is the uniform-space completeness predicate, with `Metric.complete_of_cauchySeq_tendsto` giving the sequential metric form.

annotations.label: Completion exists

anchor.snippet: it is possible to construct a complete metric space

annotations.note: `UniformSpace.Completion` constructs a complete space containing a dense copy of the original (with a `MetricSpace` instance for metric spaces).

annotations.label: Real numbers are complete

anchor.snippet: are complete under the metric induced by the usual absolute value

annotations.note: `Real.instCompleteSpace` is the instance proving ℝ is complete under its standard metric.

annotations.label: Discrete metric space

anchor.snippet: Any Cauchy sequence of elements of X must be constant beyond some fixed point

annotations.note: No named lemma states that a Cauchy sequence in a discrete space is eventually constant.

annotations.label: Rationals not complete

anchor.snippet: are not complete (for the usual distance)

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `¬ CompleteSpace ℚ` statement for the usual metric.

annotations.label: Babylonian square root of 2

anchor.snippet: however it converges to the irrational square root of 2

annotations.note: The Babylonian/Heron recursive sequence converging to √2 is not formalized as a named result.

annotations.label: Fibonacci ratios to golden ratio

anchor.snippet: of ratios of consecutive Fibonacci numbers which, if it converges at all

annotations.note: Mathlib has Binet's formula and `goldenRatio` but no theorem that the ratio fib(n+1)/fib(n) tends to φ.

annotations.label: Irrational exp, sin, cos values

anchor.snippet: The values of the exponential, sine and cosine functions

annotations.note: Irrationality of exp/sin/cos at nonzero rational arguments is not formalized (Niven's `irrational_cos_rat_mul_pi` is a different statement).

annotations.label: Open interval not complete

anchor.snippet: is not a complete space: there is a sequence

annotations.note: No named Mathlib result asserts that an open interval fails to be complete.

annotations.label: Convergent implies Cauchy

anchor.snippet: Every convergent sequence (with limit s , say) is a Cauchy sequence

annotations.note: `Filter.Tendsto.cauchySeq` shows any sequence tending to a limit is a `CauchySeq`.

annotations.label: Cauchy sequences are bounded

anchor.snippet: is bounded (since for some N , all terms of the sequence

annotations.note: `CauchySeq.isBounded_range` proves the range of a Cauchy sequence is bounded (also `cauchySeq_bdd`).

annotations.label: Convergent subsequence implies convergence

anchor.snippet: a Cauchy sequence which has a convergent subsequence with limit s is itself convergent

annotations.note: `tendsto_nhds_of_cauchySeq_of_subseq` shows a Cauchy sequence with a convergent subsequence converges to the same limit.

annotations.label: Completeness of the real numbers

anchor.snippet: Every Cauchy sequence of real numbers is bounded, hence by Bolzano–Weierstrass has a convergent subsequence

annotations.note: `Real.instCompleteSpace` is exactly the completeness of ℝ (every Cauchy sequence converges).

annotations.label: Series convergence via partial sums

anchor.snippet: is considered to be convergent if and only if the sequence of partial sums

annotations.note: `Summable.hasSum_iff_tendsto_nat` equates `HasSum f m` with convergence of partial sums `∑_{i<n} f i`, but only under a summability hypothesis.

anchors.snippet: is considered to be convergent if and only if the sequence of partial sums

annotations.label: Uniformly continuous maps preserve Cauchy

anchor.snippet: is a uniformly continuous map between the metric spaces M and N

annotations.note: `UniformContinuous.comp_cauchySeq` shows a uniformly continuous map sends Cauchy sequences to Cauchy sequences.

annotations.label: Sum and product of Cauchy sequences

anchor.snippet: are two Cauchy sequences in the rational, real or complex numbers

annotations.note: `CauchySeq.mul` (with `CauchySeq.add`, and the `Add`/`Mul` instances on `CauSeq`) gives closure of Cauchy sequences under sum and product in ℚ/ℝ/ℂ.

annotations.label: Cauchy sequence in a topological vector space

anchor.snippet: There is also a concept of Cauchy sequence for a topological vector space

annotations.note: A TVS carries a canonical uniformity (`IsUniformAddGroup`), so `CauchySeq` directly specializes to the TVS Cauchy notion.

annotations.label: Cauchy sequence in a topological group

anchor.snippet: is a Cauchy sequence if for every open neighbourhood

annotations.note: A (uniform) topological group is a uniform space, so `CauchySeq` captures the neighborhood-of-identity Cauchy condition.

annotations.label: Equivalence relation on Cauchy sequences

anchor.snippet: This relation is an equivalence relation

annotations.note: `CauSeq.equiv` provides a `Setoid` (equivalence relation) on Cauchy sequences, but only for absolute-value Cauchy sequences in a ring rather than general topological groups.

annotations.label: Cauchy sequence in a group

anchor.snippet: There is also a concept of Cauchy sequence in a group

annotations.note: The combinatorial group Cauchy notion via a decreasing chain of finite-index subgroups is not formalized.

annotations.label: Equivalence to topological group Cauchy

anchor.snippet: Technically, this is the same thing as a topological group Cauchy sequence for a particular choice of topology

annotations.note: The identification of group-filtration Cauchy sequences with topological-group ones is not formalized.

annotations.label: Completion as factor group

anchor.snippet: of such Cauchy sequences forms a group (for the componentwise product)

annotations.note: `CauSeq.Completion.Cauchy` builds the completion as the quotient of Cauchy sequences by null sequences, but in the ring/absolute-value setting rather than the general group-filtration one.

annotations.label: Completion is inverse limit

anchor.snippet: One can then show that this completion is isomorphic to the inverse limit

annotations.note: The general isomorphism between the group-filtration completion and the inverse limit G/H_r is not formalized.

annotations.label: p-adic completion of integers

anchor.snippet: An example of this construction familiar in number theory and algebraic geometry is the construction of the

annotations.note: `PadicInt.lift` realizes ℤ_p via the inverse-limit universal property over the compatible quotients `ZMod (p^n)`, capturing the p-adic completion of the integers.

annotations.label: Cofinal sequence gives canonical completion

anchor.snippet: is a cofinal sequence (that is, any normal subgroup of finite index contains some

annotations.note: The cofinality-yields-canonical-completion statement for finite-index subgroups is not formalized.

annotations.label: Cauchy sequence in a hyperreal continuum

anchor.snippet: The sequence is Cauchy if and only if for every infinite H and K

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Hyperreal` and infinitesimal proximity but not this hyperreal characterization of Cauchy sequences.

annotations.label: Cauchy completion of a category

anchor.snippet: Krause (2020) introduced a notion of Cauchy completion of a category .

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the Karoubi (idempotent) completion, which coincides with the Cauchy completion for ordinary (Set-enriched) categories, but not Krause's exact framing.
